title: When to use Gradio
question: When should AIWF use Gradio instead of a full frontend?
answer: Use Gradio when the tool is Python-first, local-first, prototype-heavy, or built around AI components like images, video, files, chat, and tables. Use a full frontend when the app needs production-grade routing, offline desktop packaging, complex user accounts, or deep custom UI behavior.

title: Interface vs Blocks
question: Should this app use gr.Interface or gr.Blocks?
answer: Use Interface for one function with fixed inputs and outputs. Use Blocks for real AIWF tools with tabs, settings, logs, multiple actions, chained callbacks, image/mask controls, or app-like workflows.

title: ChatInterface use
question: When should I use ChatInterface or Chatbot?
answer: Use ChatInterface or Chatbot when conversation history is central: local LLM chat, RAG tutor, workflow helper, prompt coach, or agent-style assistant. For non-chat task apps, use Blocks.

title: Queue long tasks
question: How should Gradio handle long AI generation jobs?
answer: Use queue-friendly design, visible progress/status/log outputs, and run manifests. Avoid callbacks that appear frozen. Long tasks should expose current stage, input settings, output paths, and errors.

title: State safety
question: What is the common Gradio state mistake?
answer: The common mistake is putting user-specific values in global Python variables. Global state is shared across users. Use session state for per-user chat history and selections; use browser state only for non-secret preferences.

title: Filesystem safety
question: What should Gradio apps avoid when handling files?
answer: Avoid broad filesystem exposure, unrestricted file browsing, accidental serving of private folders, and public share links for sensitive local tools. Use explicit input/output folders and path allowlists.

title: Gradio plus ComfyUI
question: How should Gradio and ComfyUI work together?
answer: Gradio should be the beginner-facing task control panel. ComfyUI should remain the graph execution engine. Workflow JSON remains source of truth, while Gradio collects inputs, selects presets, calls the API, and displays results/logs.

title: Inpainting UI shell
question: What should a Gradio inpainting UI include?
answer: Include source image, mask/canvas, prompt, negative prompt, model preset, denoise, seed, crop/stitch options, run button, result image, comparison gallery, saved output path, and error log.

title: Dataset QA shell
question: What should a Gradio dataset QA tool include?
answer: Include folder input, image gallery, caption viewer/editor, metadata table, approve/reject tags, search/filter, duplicate warnings, and export to CSV/JSONL manifest.

title: Beginner UX rule
question: What is the AIWF beginner UX rule for Gradio?
answer: Expose common settings clearly, hide dangerous/advanced settings behind accordions, show what will be changed before mutating files, and always provide logs that a beginner can paste into support.

title: Model loading boundary
question: Where should model loading live in a Gradio app?
answer: Keep model/service loading outside layout code but controlled by an explicit service layer. Cache heavy objects deliberately, expose reload buttons, and avoid reloading models on every UI event.

title: Flagging and feedback
question: How can Gradio support AIWF QA loops?
answer: Use flagging/logging to capture interesting failures, bad generations, prompt/result pairs, and tester notes. Store flagged samples in a reviewable directory with CSV/JSONL metadata.

topic: Gradio as an API control surface
question_patterns: how do I wrap a CLI with Gradio
how do I make a Gradio UI for local model APIs
should I put a Gradio UI over ComfyUI or Ollama
answer_intent: Explain Gradio as a human control surface over CLI/API tools with logs, safe defaults, and progress visibility.
canonical_summary: Gradio is useful when direct CLI/API use becomes too sharp for the intended user. Wrap model selection, inputs, outputs, logs, progress, and safety controls.
